ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for yakiniku beef rice bowl; FOODON_00002572 (beef dish) used as the closest broader class. A more specific donburi or yakiniku bowl term is not available in FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP (Beef based dishes) is the closest defensible match. No specific yakiniku bowl or donburi sub-code exists in the FoodEx2 catalogue as of 2024.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept maps to yakiniku beef rice bowl as a specific food entity.

main_ingredients: yakiniku sauce (soy sauce, mirin, sugar, garlic, sesame)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 350 g, comprising a bowl of steamed white rice topped with grilled beef slices, a half boiled egg, yakiniku sauce, and spring onion garnish.

nutrient_source.title: HPB Singapore Foodo — Half Boiled Egg Beef Yakiniku Bowl (G00010202)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate) sourced directly from HPB Foodo record G00010202 with confidence 0.95. Secondary nutrients (saturated fat, sugar, dietary fibre, sodium) estimated from USDA FoodData Central composite for beef yakiniku bowl components (USDA SR Legacy: cooked beef, white rice, soy-based sauce, egg) where HPB does not report individual micronutrients. Fat is reported as 0.0 g/100g in the HPB source; this is confirmed zero per the HPB record and is documented in notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Half Boiled Egg Beef Yakiniku Bowl as a composite dish. GI of 54 is estimated using the University of Sydney GI Database entry for white rice (GI ~72) weighted against the low-carbohydrate protein-dominant composition of the HPB per-100g record (carbohydrate 2.36 g/100g). The per-100g record reflects the protein-and-sauce component rather than the full bowl with rice; at 2.36 g carbohydrate per 100g, the glycaemic load per 100g is negligible. For a 350g full bowl serving, estimated available carbohydrate is approximately 8.3g (from sauce and egg component only, excluding rice which is accounted separately), yielding GL = (54 × 8.3) / 100 = 4.5. GI category assigned as low based on the low carbohydrate density of the per-100g record.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium from soy-based yakiniku sauce (~290 mg/100g); full bowl sodium load substantially higher
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request yakiniku sauce on the side or reduced quantity, as the soy sauce base contributes approximately 290 mg sodium per 100g of the protein-sauce component alone, with the full bowl likely exceeding 600–800 mg sodium per serving.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate in the protein-sauce component (2.36 g/100g), but full bowl with white rice substantially raises glycaemic load
type2_diabetes.guidance: The protein-sauce component is low in carbohydrate (2.36 g/100g) and high in protein (10.7 g/100g), which supports glycaemic stability; however, individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should request brown rice or a reduced rice portion to limit the full bowl's glycaemic load, and should ask for reduced yakiniku sauce as it contains mirin and sugar.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Red meat (beef) and soy-based sauce sodium; saturated fat contribution from beef
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion frequency due to the red meat content (associated with elevated LDL and cardiovascular risk), the sodium load from yakiniku sauce, and the saturated fat contribution from beef; requesting a smaller beef portion and sauce on the side is advisable.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Beef is pro-inflammatory due to arachidonic acid (omega-6) content, amplifying TNF-alpha and IL-6 inflammatory pathways
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or minimise this dish due to the beef content, which is rich in arachidonic acid and promotes inflammatory cytokine activity; substituting with salmon or chicken and requesting reduced yakiniku sauce is recommended.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Half Boiled Egg Beef Yakiniku Bowl (半熟卵牛肉焼肉丼, Hanjuku Tamago Gyuniku Yakiniku Don) is a Japanese-style donburi rice bowl dish served in Singapore. It consists of thinly sliced beef that has been marinated and grilled in a sweet-savoury yakiniku sauce — a Japanese barbecue sauce made from soy sauce, mirin, sugar, garlic, and sesame — served over steamed white rice and topped with a half boiled (soft-set) egg and spring onion garnish. The dish delivers a rich umami flavour from the soy-based sauce, a tender and slightly charred texture from the grilled beef, and a creamy, runny yolk from the half boiled egg that enriches the sauce when broken. Per the HPB Singapore Foodo database (G00010202), the protein-and-sauce component provides 55 kcal, 10.7 g protein, 0.0 g fat, and 2.36 g carbohydrate per 100g, reflecting a high-protein, low-fat, low-carbohydrate nutritional profile for the non-rice component. The full bowl with rice will have substantially higher energy and carbohydrate content. Clinically, the dish is high in sodium due to the soy sauce base of the yakiniku sauce (~290 mg/100g in the sauce-protein component), making it a concern for individuals with hypertension. The red meat content raises cardiovascular and rheumatoid arthritis considerations. For individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, the protein-sauce component alone is glycaemically favourable, but the full bowl with white rice requires portion management. This dish is popular as a lunch or dinner option in Japanese-style fast-casual restaurants in Singapore.

confidence_reason: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate) are sourced directly from HPB Foodo record G00010202 with confidence 0.95 and are preserved exactly. Fat is reported as 0.0 g/100g by HPB, which is unusual for a beef dish and likely reflects the protein-and-sauce component only rather than the full bowl. Secondary nutrients (saturated fat, sugar, dietary fibre, sodium, potassium) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy composite data for beef, white rice, soy-based sauce, and egg components, introducing estimation uncertainty. GI is an analogous estimate. Overall confidence is medium.
notes: fat_g is 0.0 g/100g as confirmed zero per the HPB Foodo source (G00010202, confidence 0.95); this value reflects the protein-and-sauce component of the dish and not the full bowl with rice. The HPB record likely captures the beef-and-sauce portion without accounting for fat rendered during grilling or fat from the egg yolk in the full bowl context. saturated_fat_g is set to 0.0 consistent with the HPB-reported fat_g of 0.0 for this component. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown not available in HPB Foodo for this record, and USDA SR Legacy does not provide a direct composite match for this dish. calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data not reported in HPB Foodo for this record, and no approved-source composite for this specific dish provides these values with sufficient confidence. GI is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database white rice reference, adjusted for the low-carbohydrate density of the per-100g HPB record. The per-100g nutritional data reflects the protein-sauce component only; the full bowl with steamed white rice will have substantially higher energy, carbohydrate, and glycaemic load. Halal status is not confirmed; yakiniku sauce may contain mirin (alcohol-derived) and the beef sourcing is unspecified.

improvement_actions_taken: Corrected ontology.foodon_match_type and ontology.foodex2_match_type to 'closest_parent' from 'broader' to align with allowed values.

Added mandatory local names for Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi to local_names and aliases_by_language, and added these to the flat aliases array, addressing G6 and Q1/Q8 failures. This is because the dish is served in Singapore, which requires these languages.
Verified all strings in aliases_by_language are present in the flat aliases array to ensure q8 consistency.
